Video frame interpolation (VFI) is a classical video processing problem where recent advances in VFI methods have been continuously improving the interpolation quality.
A transformer-based VFI architecture is proposed for processing both source and target frames in a unified framework for improving interpolation quality.
The proposed transformer-based VFI architecture compensates motion through a tightly integrated optical flow estimation and cross-backward warping.
The model improves over the current state-of-the-art as supported by extensive quantitative experiments and a user study.
The model predicts the interpolation uncertainty similar to approaches for artifact detection and adaptive sampling, which is important for usage in a production context.
Uncertainty estimation is used to identify problematic frames automatically in the production context.
Uncertainty estimation benefits Computer Graphics applications by determining frame patches without sufficient quality for rendering.
Frame patches are determined to not have sufficient quality in the context of Computer Graphics applications.
The transformer-based model in the production context helps render patches from the middle frame more naturally.
Rendered patches naturally fit in the same unified VFI framework in the context of the production environment.
Rendered patches from the middle frame achieve high quality levels at a fraction of the cost of rendering the full middle frame.
The paradigm in the production context is more compatible with current production renderers compared to CG specialized VFI works.
The motion-based VFI method in the context treats input and target frames similarly through a transformer-based architecture using masks.
The model achieves state-of-the-art performance in both quantitative experiments and a user study.
The output's uncertainty estimation subtask in the context can be beneficial for achieving better quality results in rendered content.
Frame interpolation in the context has been surpassed by learning-based methods compared to classical approaches using optical flow and image warping.

edge: Direct, phase, and kernel based prediction methods are reviewed in the context of frame interpolation.

node_2: regions of target frame with insufficient quality
edge: Using error estimates to find regions of the target frame with insufficient quality in order to render those areas and pass them to the network for improvement.
Most common renderers should be able to operate on a subset of rectangular tiles without a significant overhead.
Selecting tiles with the highest expected error and using them in the second interpolation pass.

The proposed VFI method incorporates optical flow motion compensation, deep feature extraction, error estimation, and shared frame processing in a transformer-based architecture.
The novel uncertainty-guided approach for animated content production greatly reduces the cost of rendering while maintaining a high visual quality as shown in experiments.
